name: Supplier Concentration Mitigation
consequences: Pulling this lever reduces reliance on Apple and Google by diversifying platform integrity signals, but it may increase integration overhead and delay procurement cycles. It forces stakeholders to evaluate alternative integrity APIs, potentially raising certification costs. Downstream, it could expose the system to new vendor risks if alternatives are not mature.
options: Mandate a comparative assessment of alternative platform integrity providers, requiring each to meet the same security criteria as Apple and Google.
Introduce a phased migration plan that gradually replaces Apple/Google signals with open‑source integrity checks, allowing overlap and risk mitigation.
Establish a joint task force with academia and industry to develop a proprietary, platform‑agnostic integrity framework that can be certified by the Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
review: Core tension: balancing supplier diversification against procurement speed; options miss the risk of regulatory delays and lack a concrete fallback schedule.

name: Platform‑Neutral Procurement Framing
consequences: Pulling this lever reframes procurement language to require platform neutrality, potentially broadening vendor options but may trigger resistance from incumbent suppliers and extend tender timelines. It could also raise the bar for compliance, requiring more rigorous testing of non‑Apple/Google solutions. Downstream, it may lead to higher upfront costs but improve long‑term resilience.
options: Draft procurement specifications that explicitly prohibit exclusive reliance on Apple or Google platform integrity APIs, mandating alternative verification methods.
Propose a modular procurement approach where core identity services are split into platform‑agnostic components and platform‑dependent extensions.
Advocate for a procurement clause that rewards vendors who provide documented fallback authentication pathways independent of mobile OS ecosystems.
review: Core tension: ensuring procurement neutrality while avoiding vendor lock‑in; options fail to address the challenge of convincing stakeholders that alternative solutions meet existing security standards.

name: Resilience‑Oriented Policy Advocacy
consequences: Pulling this lever elevates resilience as a primary policy objective, potentially shifting budget allocations toward contingency planning but may dilute focus on immediate service delivery. It could also create friction with agencies prioritizing cost efficiency. Downstream, it may lead to stronger continuity plans but risk slower adoption of new technologies.
options: Launch a policy brief that quantifies the risk of platform outages and recommends mandatory resilience metrics in all digital identity contracts.
Organize a series of workshops with public sector IT leaders to embed resilience KPIs into procurement evaluation criteria.
Create a cross‑ministerial task force that monitors platform dependency trends and publishes quarterly resilience reports.
review: Core tension: prioritizing resilience over speed; options overlook the need for measurable, enforceable metrics that can be audited by regulators.

name: Technical Demonstrator Credibility
consequences: Pulling this lever provides tangible evidence of fallback viability, potentially accelerating stakeholder buy‑in but may divert resources from policy work. It could also expose technical gaps that require additional development. Downstream, it may increase confidence but also raise expectations for production readiness.
options: Develop a mobile demonstrator using GrapheneOS that authenticates via FIDO2/WebAuthn against a simulated identity provider, showcasing platform neutrality.
Build a browser‑based fallback prototype that leverages hardware tokens and OpenID Connect, illustrating non‑OS‑dependent access.
Integrate a hybrid demonstrator that combines mobile and hardware token authentication, demonstrating flexibility across device ecosystems.
review: Core tension: balancing demonstrator depth with resource constraints; options miss the risk of overpromising production readiness and underestimating integration complexity.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Strengthening
consequences: Pulling this lever expands the coalition, potentially increasing influence but may dilute focus and create coordination challenges. It could also lead to conflicting priorities among members. Downstream, it may improve advocacy outcomes but risk slower decision‑making.
options: Invite privacy advocacy groups, academic researchers, and contingency planners to a quarterly steering committee that sets joint advocacy priorities.
Establish a digital identity advisory board that includes representatives from affected ministries, civil society, and industry experts.
Create a public‑facing platform where coalition members can share research findings, policy drafts, and stakeholder feedback in real time.
review: Core tension: broadening coalition versus maintaining strategic focus; options overlook the need for clear decision‑making authority within the coalition.

name: EU Standards Alignment
consequences: Pulling this lever aligns Danish policy with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards, potentially easing cross‑border interoperability but may require adjustments to national procurement rules. It could also expose Denmark to EU regulatory scrutiny. Downstream, it may strengthen Denmark’s position in EU policy but risk delays in national implementation.
options: Submit technical contributions to the European Digital Identity Wallet working group that advocate for platform‑neutral fallback mechanisms.
Draft a national policy brief that maps Danish procurement requirements to EU Digital Identity Wallet interoperability criteria.
Organize a joint EU‑Danish workshop that explores harmonizing fallback authentication standards across member states.
review: Core tension: aligning with EU standards while preserving national autonomy; options miss the challenge of reconciling differing regulatory timelines.

name: Platform Integrity Signal Decoupling
consequences: Pulling this lever would shift the trust model from platform‑controlled integrity signals to a self‑contained verification layer, reducing reliance on Apple/Google. This would increase the complexity of the authentication stack and require new certification pathways, potentially delaying rollout. It also opens the door for alternative device‑trust providers, but may fragment the ecosystem if not coordinated.
options: Develop an open‑source device‑trust module that validates hardware integrity without platform APIs, and integrate it into the existing MitID workflow.
Create a cross‑platform integrity verification service that aggregates signals from multiple OS vendors, and embed it as a fallback in the authentication flow.
Establish a hardware‑based attestation enclave that operates independently of OS integrity checks, and expose it through a standardized API for identity providers.
review: Decoupling platform integrity signals reduces vendor lock‑in but introduces certification overhead; the options overlook the need for a unified attestation standard that all stakeholders can adopt.

name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Integration
consequences: Implementing a fallback path would provide continuity during platform outages, but it may increase user friction if the fallback requires additional device or credential steps. It also demands new policy language in procurement documents and could slow the adoption of the primary MitID flow if not clearly differentiated.
options: Design a browser‑based WebAuthn fallback that users can access via any modern browser, and publish a step‑by‑step guide for public sector portals.
Introduce a hardware‑token fallback that can be issued to citizens, and integrate token verification into the existing authentication backend.
Implement a short‑lived, QR‑code‑based authentication flow that can be triggered when mobile OS integrity signals are unavailable, and ensure it meets eIDAS security requirements.
review: Providing a fallback path enhances resilience but may dilute user experience; the options fail to address how to incentivize users to adopt the fallback when the primary path is available.

name: Procurement Process Restructuring
consequences: Restructuring procurement to include platform‑neutral clauses would force vendors to design for multiple OS environments, potentially raising development costs. It could also lengthen tender cycles and create uncertainty for suppliers accustomed to a single‑platform focus.
options: Mandate that all future AltID procurement documents require explicit platform‑neutral authentication modules, and audit compliance through a dedicated oversight committee.
Introduce a phased procurement approach where initial pilots test platform‑neutral solutions, and successful vendors receive preferential terms in subsequent full‑scale contracts.
Create a procurement sandbox that allows vendors to demonstrate platform‑neutral capabilities before formal tendering, and publish the sandbox results as a public benchmark.
review: Restructuring procurement promotes neutrality but may increase administrative burden; the options ignore the risk of vendor attrition if the new requirements are perceived as too onerous.

name: Stakeholder Engagement Amplification
consequences: Amplifying stakeholder engagement would broaden the coalition, increasing political pressure on decision makers, but it may also dilute focus and create conflicting priorities. It could also extend the project timeline due to coordination overhead.
options: Organize quarterly cross‑sector workshops that bring together civil society, academia, industry, and government to co‑create platform‑neutral policy drafts.
Launch a public consultation portal that collects structured feedback on fallback authentication scenarios, and use the data to refine technical proposals.
Establish a formal advisory board composed of representatives from each stakeholder group, and convene it monthly to review progress and adjust strategy.
review: Expanding engagement increases legitimacy but risks fragmentation; the options overlook mechanisms to reconcile divergent stakeholder interests into a unified advocacy agenda.

name: Regulatory Framework Re‑engineering
consequences: Re‑engineering the regulatory framework to embed platform neutrality would create a durable policy foundation, but it may trigger extensive legal review cycles and require alignment with EU eIDAS provisions. It could also create uncertainty for existing operators during the transition.
options: Draft a regulatory amendment that defines platform‑neutral authentication as a mandatory requirement for all public digital identity services, and submit it to the Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
Propose a transitional regulatory pathway that allows existing services to adopt platform neutrality over a defined grace period, while mandating new services to comply from inception.
Develop a regulatory sandbox that permits experimental platform‑neutral solutions to operate under provisional approval, and use the results to inform permanent policy.
review: Re‑engineering regulation ensures long‑term change but may stall due to legislative inertia; the options miss the need for a clear enforcement mechanism once the policy is adopted.

name: Cross‑Industry Resilience Modeling
consequences: Modeling resilience across industries would provide evidence of the benefits of platform neutrality, but it may require data sharing agreements that are difficult to secure. It also risks over‑generalizing findings that may not translate directly to the public sector.
options: Conduct a comparative resilience analysis of banking, healthcare, and public administration sectors, quantifying the impact of platform lock‑in on service continuity.
Build a simulation model that projects outage scenarios for mobile OS vendors, and evaluate how fallback authentication mitigates downtime across multiple sectors.
Create a cross‑industry case study repository that documents real‑world incidents where platform dependency caused service disruptions, and use it to advocate for neutrality.
review: Cross‑industry modeling offers persuasive data but may not capture sector‑specific nuances; the options fail to address how to translate findings into actionable procurement language.

name: Mobile OS Dependency Auditing
consequences: Pulling this lever will produce a detailed audit of current mobile OS dependency across all citizen‑facing services, revealing the extent of reliance on Apple and Google integrity signals. The audit will expose hidden contractual clauses and technical lock‑in points, but it may also surface legacy code that cannot be refactored without significant cost, potentially delaying procurement timelines.
options: Conduct a comprehensive code‑level dependency scan across all MitID‑connected applications, mapping every external API call to Apple or Google services, and publish the findings in a public report to pressure vendors.

Engage a third‑party security consultancy to perform a penetration‑testing exercise that specifically targets OS‑level integrity checks, documenting how a single OS policy change could cascade into service outages.

Deploy a lightweight sandbox environment that emulates both iOS and Android integrity APIs, allowing developers to experiment with alternative verification mechanisms without altering production code.
review: Technical dependency mapping highlights OS reliance, yet the options ignore the contractual lock‑in that would require renegotiation of vendor agreements, limiting the audit’s impact.

name: Cross‑Platform Credential Portability
consequences: Pulling this lever will push for a design that allows citizen credentials to be stored and verified across multiple operating systems, reducing single‑vendor lock‑in. The shift will increase interoperability but may raise privacy concerns as credentials move between platforms, potentially triggering additional regulatory scrutiny.
options: Standardize on a FIDO2/WebAuthn credential format that can be exported and imported across iOS, Android, and web browsers, and develop a migration toolkit for users.
Introduce a federated credential broker that abstracts platform differences, enabling a single credential to be presented to any service regardless of underlying OS.
Pilot a cross‑platform credential wallet using open‑source hardware tokens, demonstrating that physical tokens can bridge OS gaps without relying on app‑store distribution.
review: The focus on credential portability assumes technical feasibility but neglects the user‑experience friction of migrating credentials, which could erode adoption.

name: Digital Sovereignty Legal Framing
consequences: Pulling this lever will reframe the dependency issue as a national sovereignty concern, potentially unlocking legal mechanisms for procurement neutrality. This framing may accelerate policy adoption but could also provoke diplomatic pushback from platform vendors, risking trade‑related retaliation.
options: Draft a legal memorandum that cites Danish constitutional provisions on digital sovereignty to argue for mandatory platform‑neutral procurement clauses in public contracts.
Propose a legislative amendment to the Digital Identity Act that requires any national identity system to include a certified fallback authentication path, citing EU eIDAS obligations.
Engage with the European Parliament to co‑author a resolution that positions Denmark as a leader in digital sovereignty, leveraging EU funding for platform‑neutral research.
review: Legal framing can mobilize policy but may overlook the practical procurement constraints and the need for technical feasibility evidence, limiting its persuasive power.

name: Stakeholder Trust Calibration
consequences: Pulling this lever will systematically assess and calibrate trust levels among key stakeholders, ensuring that advocacy efforts are aligned with their risk appetite. The calibration may reveal divergent priorities, forcing the project to negotiate trade‑offs between rapid deployment and thorough security vetting.
options: Conduct a structured stakeholder survey that quantifies trust in platform vendors versus national identity providers, and publish a confidence index to guide policy messaging.
Host a series of moderated workshops where civil‑society groups, privacy advocates, and procurement officials jointly evaluate risk scenarios and rank acceptable fallback mechanisms.
Deploy a real‑time feedback dashboard that tracks stakeholder sentiment during public consultations, allowing the project team to adjust messaging in near‑real time.
review: Trust calibration focuses on perception but may miss the underlying technical dependencies that actually drive risk, limiting its effectiveness in shaping procurement decisions.

name: Resilience Benchmarking Protocol
consequences: Pulling this lever will establish a set of resilience metrics for Danish digital identity, enabling objective comparison of fallback options. The protocol will raise the bar for future procurements but may also increase the administrative burden on vendors and delay procurement cycles.
options: Define a resilience scorecard that measures uptime, recovery time, and vendor diversity for each authentication pathway, and require vendors to submit scores during tender evaluation.
Create a simulation framework that models service disruption scenarios (e.g., OS policy change, supply chain outage) and evaluates fallback performance against baseline metrics.
Publish a best‑practice guide that outlines resilience testing procedures for identity providers, encouraging voluntary compliance before formal procurement.
review: Benchmarking raises standards but may create a compliance trap where vendors focus on metrics rather than genuine resilience, potentially stifling innovation.

name: Alternative Push Notification Architecture
consequences: Pulling this lever will explore push‑notification mechanisms that do not rely on Apple or Google services, reducing platform lock‑in for critical alerts. The new architecture may improve resilience but could increase development complexity and require new security certifications.
options: Design a server‑side push system that uses Web Push API with encrypted payloads, allowing browsers and custom clients to receive alerts without OS‑level mediation.
Implement a lightweight MQTT broker on a trusted national server, enabling device‑to‑device notifications that bypass mobile OS push services.
Prototype a Bluetooth Low Energy beacon network that delivers time‑sensitive notifications to nearby devices, providing an offline fallback when network connectivity is lost.
review: Exploring alternative push channels reduces OS dependence but may overlook the need for end‑to‑end encryption guarantees, risking security gaps in the fallback path.
